objectives: Convert confidently between denary, binary and hexadecimal, and represent signed integers using two's complement.
Apply binary addition, subtraction and logical/arithmetic shifts, identifying overflow and the arithmetic effect of each shift.
Explain floating-point representation (mantissa and exponent), normalisation, and how character sets such as ASCII and Unicode encode characters.

retrieval.q: How many bits are represented by one hexadecimal digit?
retrieval.a: 4 bits (one nibble); two hex digits make one byte.

retrieval.q: In an 8-bit two's complement number, what is the place value of the most significant bit?
Name a primitive data type that holds True or False.
Interleaving (1.2): What does a compiler do, and how does it differ from an interpreter?
retrieval.a: A compiler translates the whole source program into object/machine code in one go before execution; an interpreter translates and executes line by line.

retrieval.q: Interleaving (1.4.3): What is the output of a two-input XOR gate when both inputs are 1?
retrieval.a: 0 (XOR outputs 1 only when the inputs differ).

keywords.def: Signed-integer representation in which the most significant bit has a negative place value; negate a number by inverting all bits and adding 1.
Base-16 number system (0-9, A-F) used as a compact, lossless shorthand for binary; one hex digit equals four bits.
An error where the result of a calculation is too large to fit in the available number of bits.
The significant-figures part of a floating-point number; determines its precision.
The power-of-two part of a floating-point number; determines its range.
Adjusting a float so the mantissa begins with the first significant bit (0.1... for positive, 1.0... for negative) to maximise precision.
A right shift that copies the sign bit in from the left, dividing a signed number by powers of two.
A mapping of characters to unique binary codes, such as ASCII (7-bit, 128 characters) or Unicode (multi-byte, all languages plus emoji).

content.heading: Number bases and conversion
points: Denary to binary: subtract the largest place value that fits and repeat, or divide by 2 reading remainders bottom-up.
Binary to hex: split into nibbles from the right (pad the left with 0s) and convert each nibble.
Hex is used because it is a compact, error-free shorthand for binary in addresses, colours and machine code; it does not save memory.

content.heading: Signed integers and two's complement
points: 8-bit two's complement range is -128 to +127; an MSB of 1 means the number is negative.
To negate, invert all bits then add 1 (e.g. +5 = 00000101 -> invert 11111010 -> +1 = 11111011 = -5).
Subtraction A - B is done as A + (-B), adding the two's complement of B and discarding the final carry out of the MSB.

content.heading: Binary shifts and overflow
points: Logical left shift by n multiplies an unsigned number by 2^n; logical right shift divides by 2^n.
Arithmetic right shift copies the sign bit in from the left, dividing a signed number by 2^n.
Bits shifted off the end are lost, and a left shift can cause overflow if a 1 is pushed beyond the available bits.

content.heading: Floating-point representation
points: Value = mantissa x 2^exponent; the mantissa sets precision and the exponent sets range, both stored in two's complement.
Moving the point right decreases the exponent; moving it left increases it, with the value unchanged.
Fixed total bits force a trade-off: more mantissa bits give more precision but less range; more exponent bits give more range but less precision.

content.heading: Character sets
points: ASCII uses 7 bits for 128 characters ('A'=65, 'a'=97, '0'=48); extended ASCII uses 8 bits for 256.
Unicode uses more bits (UTF-8 is 1-4 bytes) to represent over a million code points covering all languages and emoji.
The first 128 Unicode code points match ASCII, so ASCII is a subset and Unicode is backward-compatible.

worked_example.title: 8-bit two's complement subtraction: 45 - 28
steps: Add 00101101 + 11100100 = 1 00010001 (a carry is produced out of the MSB).
Discard the final carry out of the MSB, leaving 00010001.

task: Convert 217 to 8-bit unsigned binary, then convert your answer to hexadecimal. Next convert the binary 10110110 to hex, and the hex B7 to denary, showing your method for each.
challenge: Normalise the 8-bit signed float with mantissa 0.0110100 and exponent 0011 (4-bit two's complement): give the normalised mantissa and new exponent, then prove the value is unchanged by evaluating both forms in denary.

exit_ticket.q: What two steps negate a number in two's complement?
exit_ticket.a: Invert all the bits, then add 1.

exit_ticket.q: In subtraction by two's complement, what happens to the final carry out of the MSB?
With a fixed number of bits, what is the effect of giving more bits to the mantissa?
exit_ticket.a: Greater precision (more significant figures) but a smaller range, because fewer bits remain for the exponent.
